one_line_takeaway: A 20-dimensional spectral signature (per-projection singular-value concentration, entropy, norm, and kurtosis of LoRA weight updates) fed to a simple logistic-regression classifier achieves perfect held-out separation of backdoored vs. benign LoRA adapters across three model families, without ever running the model -- but the approach assumes a non-adaptive attacker and needs a per-architecture labeled calibration set.
key_finding: Across all three architectures (Qwen2.5-3B, Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct, Gemma-2-2B) the trained logistic-regression detector achieves 100% accuracy, 1.00 ROC-AUC, 0% FPR, and 0% FNR on held-out test adapters, with score gaps (between poisoned-min and benign-max, per the paper's threshold rule) of 0.495 (Qwen), 0.578 (Llama), and 0.779 (Gemma). No single feature or projection dominates universally: mean orientation-free univariate ROC-AUC peaks at different features per backbone (Qwen: energy concentration E=0.832; Llama: kurtosis K=0.979; Gemma: entropy H=0.823), and the weakest features are consistently σ1 and Frobenius norm. Trained-detector projection reliance is also backbone-dependent (e.g., Qwen relies most on the v-projection, R_v=0.508; Llama's reliance is distributed across q, o, k; Gemma concentrates on q and v). Layer placement matters more than LoRA rank for separability: at rank 16, composite separability rises from 0.27 (layer 3) and 0.07 (layer 9) to 0.79 (layer 21) and 0.74 (layer 27), whereas varying rank at layer 21 only moves the score from 0.61 (r=8) to 0.75 (r=32). The payload-legibility probe shows a monotonic effect of poisoning strength: the average rank of the first HACK-family token in the top-20 projected tokens drops from 21 (absent, benign) to 20.4 at 1% poisoning, 9.6 at 3%, and 4.8 at 5%, indicating stronger poisoning increasingly aligns the dominant singular direction with payload vocabulary. The cross-model geometry analysis shows architecture is the dominant global factor in feature space: mean same-model-family cosine similarity is 0.322 versus -0.154 across model families.
core_contribution: The paper proposes a static, execution-free detector for backdoored LoRA adapters that operates purely on the adapter's weight matrices (no model execution, no trigger knowledge, no training data). For each attention projection (Q, K, V, O) at a selected transformer layer, the method reconstructs the LoRA update ΔW_p from its low-rank factors via a QR-based reduced SVD (following Halko et al. 2011), then extracts five spectral-geometric statistics per projection: top singular value σ1, Frobenius norm of ΔW, singular-value energy concentration E, spectral entropy H, and kurtosis K of the update entries. Concatenating the four per-projection 5-dimensional descriptors yields a 20-dimensional signature Φ(A) per adapter, which is standardized and fed to a logistic-regression classifier with a threshold selected via either a margin rule or Youden's J statistic. The authors construct a new cross-architecture benchmark (Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct, Qwen2.5-3B, Gemma-2-2B) with matched benign/poisoned adapter banks and report perfect separation on held-out test adapters for all three backbones.
threat_model: Supply-chain setting: a third party distributes a LoRA adapter for a frozen, publicly known base model (e.g., via Hugging Face Hub). The attacker's goal is to publish a poisoned adapter that behaves normally on ordinary inputs but produces attacker-chosen output when an unknown trigger is present, while remaining useful for benign tasks. The defender (hub operator / downstream adopter) receives only the adapter weights before deployment -- no access to the original training data, trigger strings, or ability to execute the model against the adapter. The paper explicitly assumes a non-adaptive attacker with respect to the proposed detector (i.e., the attacker is not aware of, or does not optimize against, this specific spectral detector); poisoned adapters in the benchmark span multiple poisoning rates (1%, 3%, 5%) and two trigger types (rare-token 'cf' and contextual 'Important update:') rather than a single fixed attack strength.
limitations: The paper's own stated limitations: (1) the method still requires a labeled calibration set per new model family to fit standardization statistics and the decision threshold -- it is not zero-shot across architectures; (2) per-adapter screening requires QR/SVD-based feature extraction for four projections at a selected layer, a nontrivial per-adapter cost the authors explicitly retain because the signal is distributed across features/projections rather than a single cheap scalar; (3) the method assumes a non-adaptive attacker -- an adversary aware of the detector could try to regularize training to spread the malicious update more diffusely across projections/singular modes, trading backdoor effectiveness for geometric stealth, and the paper states robustness to fully adaptive attackers is an open problem; (4) the benchmark itself is small-scale relative to real adapter hubs (400 benign + 100 poisoned calibration and 50+50 test adapters per backbone, three model families in the 2-3B parameter range, two trigger families) so generalization to substantially larger models, more trigger diversity, or attacks specifically designed to spread across projections is untested; (5) the layer/rank sweep and cross-architecture geometry analyses use auxiliary probes/descriptors that are explicitly distinct from the deployed 20-d detector vector, so those interpretive results do not directly validate the deployed classifier's behavior at other operating points.

datasets_used: custom rare-token trigger ('cf') poisoned adapters at 1%/3%/5% poisoning rates
custom contextual trigger ('Important update:') poisoned adapters at 1%/3%/5% poisoning rates
quantitative_results: Detection accuracy: 100% for Qwen, Llama, and Gemma (Table 2)
FPR: 0.00, FNR: 0.00 for all three backbones
Mean orientation-free univariate ROC-AUC by feature: sigma1 0.639/0.651/0.619, ||ΔW||_F 0.606/0.597/0.570, E 0.832/0.800/0.750, H 0.820/0.748/0.823, K 0.831/0.979/0.786 (Qwen/Llama/Gemma, Table 3)
Projection importance (Qwen): R_v = 0.508, largest among q/k/v/o
Layer/rank composite separability C(l,r) at r=16: 0.27 (L3), 0.07 (L9), 0.79 (L21), 0.74 (L27); at L21: 0.61 (r=8), 0.75 (r=32)
HACK-token rank (payload legibility, Llama layer-21 o-proj): 21.0 (benign) -> 20.35 (1% poison) -> 9.59 (3% poison) -> 4.75 (5% poison)
Cross-model geometry: same-model cosine similarity avg 0.322 vs. -0.154 across models, computed over 2104 Qwen/Llama/Gemma adapters
baselines_compared: No direct empirical baseline comparison is run in this paper (e.g., PEFTGuard is discussed only in Related Work as the closest prior method, not re-implemented or benchmarked head-to-head); the paper's own detector is evaluated only against itself across ablations (feature-wise, projection-wise, layer/rank sweeps) and against a random/naive separation implicit in the perfect-accuracy result.
